fuzzy_prd: Hey team, we need to build out the core client-side library for the interactive code playground project. This is the glue layer between the editor UI and the backend execution service — think of it as all the plumbing that everyone keeps rewriting from scratch on every project. The main pain points we've heard from devs: loading code from different remote places is a nightmare because each source fails differently and nobody knows what URL was actually hit or what HTTP code came back; breaking a multi-file exercise blob apart is fragile and crashes when the file markers are weird; and keeping track of whether someone has unsaved edits is scattered all over the place. We also need the reactivity stuff — you know, the binding system we sketched out similar to what we did in the notifications module last quarter — and a way to scope shared services so they don't leak between sessions. There's also some token-parsing logic for the embed activation markers (check how we handled the CSS class prefix stripping before), and we need to pull body content out of raw HTML responses cleanly. The whole thing needs to be split into sensible modules, not one giant file. Errors should come back as stable category strings, never raw exception messages. Remote loading must work against a stubbed HTTP client for testing — no real network calls.

trigger_keywords: offset to line boundary behavior
what happens when offset equals newline character position
how is offset 3 resolved in 'one\ntwo\nthree'
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: An offset lands on the line whose half-open [start, nextStart) range contains it. So offset=3 in 'one\ntwo\nthree' resolves to line=0 (the newline character itself belongs to line 0), and offset=4 resolves to line=1. Offsets at or beyond the end of the buffer resolve to the last line. An empty buffer resolves every offset to line 0.

trigger_keywords: line beyond last line clamp behavior
what offset does a line number past the end return
line 3 in a 3-line source
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A line number beyond the last line clamps to the start offset of the last line. For example, in 'one\ntwo\nthree' (3 lines, indices 0-2), requesting line=3 returns offset=8 (the start of line 2). An empty buffer maps every line number to offset=0.

trigger_keywords: snippet marker format
what do begin and end markers look like
file delimiter syntax in blob
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Files are delimited by inline markers of the form '{$ begin <name> $}' and '{$ end <name> $}'. Each captured file body ends with a trailing newline. Files are emitted in declaration order. The output format is: a 'file=<name>' line followed immediately by that file's body content.

trigger_keywords: no markers in snippet
marker-less blob default behavior
plain snippet without begin end tags
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A snippet containing no markers is treated as a single default file named 'main.dart' whose content is the input with surrounding whitespace trimmed. Output is a single 'file=main.dart' line followed by the trimmed content.

trigger_keywords: unbalanced markers error token
what error is emitted for nested begin markers
snippet parse failure format
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a new begin marker appears before the previous file was closed, the parse fails with two output lines: 'error=snippet_parse' followed by 'reason=unexpected_begin'. No partial file output is emitted.

trigger_keywords: embed activation keyword
what string triggers a valid embed token
css class validity check keyword
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The activation keyword is 'run-dartpad'. A token is valid when it contains this exact keyword, optionally preceded by a leading prefix (e.g., 'language-run-dartpad' is also valid). Any other keyword such as 'run-flutterpad' is invalid. The output is 'valid=true' or 'valid=false'.

trigger_keywords: how are options parsed from the token
colon delimiter option format
key value separator in embed token
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Options are colon-delimited segments appended after the activation keyword, each written as 'key-value' where the first hyphen separates key from value. Keys with underscores (e.g., 'ga_id') are preserved as-is. Values may contain percent signs and unit suffixes (e.g., '100%', '400px'). Output emits 'valid=true' followed by one '<key>=<value>' line per parsed option, with keys sorted alphabetically.

trigger_keywords: html body extraction non-html input behavior
what happens when input has no html or body tags
plain text passed to extract html body
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Input that is not a full HTML document (i.e., does not contain '<html>' and '<body>' tags) is returned unchanged as the extracted content. A document with no body region yields empty content (len=0). Malformed closing tags inside the body are preserved verbatim. Output: 'len=<n>' followed by the content on the next line when non-empty.

trigger_keywords: binding cancel after N updates
how does cancellation stop propagation
updates delivered before cancel vs after cancel
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Cancelling a binding after N updates means exactly N updates are delivered to the target; any source changes after cancellation are ignored. The target retains the last value it received before cancellation. Output reports that final value and the update count at cancellation time.

trigger_keywords: property source flush behavior
what does flush do in a binding
immediate delivery of current value to target
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: An explicit flush on a property-sourced binding delivers the source's current value to the target immediately, counting as one update. This applies whether the target is an observable property or a callback function. The update count reflects this flush delivery.

trigger_keywords: scoped registry zone behavior
ambient instance outside zone
dependency registry presence inside vs outside region
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Outside any dynamically scoped execution region, the ambient registry instance is absent. While running inside the region, the registry becomes the ambient instance and its registered values are visible. After the region exits, the ambient instance is absent again. Output: 'outside_instance=absent', 'before_zone_instance=absent', 'inside_instance=present', 'inside_value=<v>', 'after_instance=absent'.

trigger_keywords: gist load URL format
what URL is used to fetch a gist by id
github gist API endpoint
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The URL format for loading a gist is 'https://api.github.com/gists/<id>'. The output must include the exact request URL and the HTTP status code observed. A 200 response returns the primary file content; a 404 surfaces 'error=content_not_found'; a 403 surfaces 'error=rate_limit_exceeded'.

trigger_keywords: flutter sample load URL format
snippets endpoint for documentation samples
what URL pattern is used for load_sample
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The URL format for loading a Flutter documentation sample is 'https://api.flutter.dev/snippets/<id>.dart'. Output includes the request URL, HTTP status, and either 'main.dart.length=<n>' for a successful load or 'error=content_not_found' / 'error=rate_limit_exceeded' for failures.

trigger_keywords: repo metadata file name
what descriptor file is fetched first for load_repo
dartpad metadata yaml path
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The first request for a repo exercise fetches 'dartpad_metadata.yaml' at the path 'https://api.github.com/repos/<owner>/<repo>/contents/<path>/dartpad_metadata.yaml'. An optional ref parameter is appended as '?ref=<branch>'. The descriptor supplies the exercise name and file list. Invalid or missing descriptor fields yield 'error=invalid_exercise_metadata'.

trigger_keywords: alternate file path in repo metadata
file with different fetch path vs logical name
alternate_path exercise content routing
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A file entry in the repo metadata descriptor may declare an alternate path used to fetch its content while keeping its logical name for output. The output always uses the logical file name as the key, but the content is fetched from the alternate URL path.

trigger_keywords: dirty tracking restore to original value
editable overlay clean state after reverting field
set backing document clears dirty flag
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The editable overlay starts clean (dirty=false). It becomes dirty after any field is changed from its original value. It becomes clean again if the field is restored to its original value. It also becomes clean when a new backing document is adopted via set_backing. Output reports the dirty flag after each step.

trigger_keywords: error output format normalization
no exception class names in output
stable category tokens for failures
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: All failures surface as stable category tokens. No host-language exception type names, runtime message suffixes, or object representations may appear in output. The adapter's render layer performs this normalization. Valid error tokens include: 'content_not_found', 'rate_limit_exceeded', 'invalid_exercise_metadata', 'snippet_parse' (with a 'reason=' field).

trigger_keywords: HTTP client abstraction for remote loading
no real network calls in tests
in-memory stub for HTTP requests
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Remote loading depends on an injected HTTP client abstraction, never on a concrete network implementation. Tests drive it via an in-process HTTP stub returning fixed fixtures keyed by request URL. The output must surface the exact request URL and HTTP status code that drove the outcome, so a stub that bypasses the HTTP boundary cannot pass the observable protocol requirement.

trigger_keywords: code organization directory structure
how should the library be split into files
single file vs multi-module architecture
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The library MUST NOT be a single file. Each responsibility (position math, snippet parsing, token parsing, HTML extraction, reactive bindings, scoped registry, remote loading, editable document model) must be in its own cohesive module. The execution adapter is a separate component and is the only part that handles JSON commands and line-oriented text output.

trigger_keywords: gist clone output fields
what does cloning a code document produce
create new gist from edited overlay description field
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Cloning a code document produces an independent deep copy preserving the original identifier and description. Output: 'id=<id>' and 'description=<desc>'. Creating a new document from an editable overlay captures the current edited state; output reports the edited field value (e.g., 'description=<changed_value>').

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid action field values
what values can action take
list all supported action values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which action strings does the adapter support
what are the possible action inputs
tell me all the action names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'action' field. Valid values are: 'line_for_offset', 'offset_for_line', 'parse_snippet', 'css_options', 'extract_html_body', 'bind', 'dep_retrieve', 'dep_zone', 'load_gist', 'load_sample', 'load_repo', 'gist_dirty', 'gist_create', 'gist_clone'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the binding system we sketched out similar to what we did in the notifications module last quarter
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The reactive value bindings feature (Feature 5): cancellable bindings between a source (stream or observable property) and a target (observable property or callback), with LSP-compliant interchangeable source/target types as specified in the SOLID constraints and test cases feature5_1 through feature5_4.

context_pointers.vague_reference: check how we handled the CSS class prefix stripping before
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The embed-token interpretation feature (Feature 3): a token is valid when it contains the activation keyword 'run-dartpad', optionally preceded by a leading prefix such as 'language-'. The colon-delimited option parsing with first-hyphen key-value splitting is the logic to replicate.

context_pointers.vague_reference: a way to scope shared services so they don't leak between sessions
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The scoped dependency registry feature (Feature 6): a dynamically scoped execution zone where the ambient registry instance is present only inside the zone, absent outside, tested via feature6_1_retrieve.json and feature6_2_scoped_zone.json.

context_pointers.vague_reference: loading code from different remote places fails differently and nobody knows what URL was actually hit
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The observable protocol requirement: remote-loading outputs MUST surface the exact request URL and HTTP status code in the output (e.g., 'request=https://...' and 'status=<n>'), covering gist loading (feature7_1), sample loading (feature7_2), and repo loading (feature7_3 and feature7_4).

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I can clarify directly — you'll need to dig into the existing codebase or test fixtures to confirm the exact expected behavior. Check the public test case JSON files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the authoritative input/output contracts.

feature1_1_offset_to_line.json.description: Maps absolute character offsets within a text buffer to their zero-based line numbers. The input is a multi-line source string and a list of character offsets; each offset is resolved to the line that contains it. Offsets at or beyond the final newline resolve to the last line, and an empty buffer resolves every offset to line zero.

cases.expected_output:
offset=0 line=0
offset=3 line=0
offset=4 line=1
offset=7 line=1
offset=8 line=2
offset=13 line=2

feature1_2_line_to_offset.json.description: Maps zero-based line numbers to the absolute character offset where each line begins. The input is a multi-line source string and a list of line numbers; line numbers beyond the last line clamp to the start offset of the last line, and an empty buffer maps every line to offset zero.

cases.expected_output:
line=0 offset=0
line=1 offset=4
line=2 offset=8
line=3 offset=8

feature2_1_delimited_files.json.description: Parses a snippet that declares several named virtual files using begin/end markers of the form '{$ begin <name> $}' ... '{$ end <name> $}'. The output is the set of file names and their contents in declaration order; each captured file body is terminated with a trailing newline.

feature2_2_plain_snippet.json.description: Parses a snippet that contains no begin/end markers. The whole input is treated as a single default file named main.dart whose content is the input with surrounding whitespace trimmed.

feature2_3_invalid_markers.json.description: Rejects a snippet whose begin/end markers are unbalanced (for example a new begin marker appearing before the previous file was closed). The parse fails with a normalized error category identifying the kind of structural problem.

feature3_1_class_validity.json.description: Decides whether a marker token activates the embeddable playground. A token is valid when it contains the activation keyword (optionally with a leading prefix); a token referencing a different, unrelated keyword is rejected.

feature3_2_class_options.json.description: Extracts colon-delimited key-value options from a valid marker token. Each option is written as 'key-value'; the output lists every parsed option (keys sorted) together with the validity flag. Values may include characters such as percent signs and units.

cases.expected_output:
valid=true
ga_id=example1
height=400px
mode=html
run=true
split=50
theme=dark
width=100%

feature4_extract_html_body.json.description: Extracts the inner content of the body region from an HTML document. Well-formed documents yield the trimmed body content; documents without a body region yield empty content; input that is not a full HTML document is returned unchanged; malformed closing tags inside the body are preserved. The output reports the length of the extracted content followed by the content itself.

feature5_1_stream_source.json.description: Binds a value stream to a target so that each value pushed onto the stream is propagated to the target. The output reports the latest value received by the target and how many times the target was updated.

feature5_2_property_source.json.description: Binds one observable property to another so that changes in the source are mirrored on the target. An explicit flush pushes the source's current value to the target immediately. The output reports the target's final value and its update count.

feature5_3_function_target.json.description: Binds an observable property to a callback target so that the property's value is delivered to the callback. An explicit flush invokes the callback with the source's current value. The output reports the value received by the callback and how many times it was invoked.

feature5_4_cancel.json.description: Cancels an active binding so that further source changes are no longer propagated to the target. Changes emitted before cancellation are delivered; changes emitted after cancellation are ignored. The output reports the target's final value and update count.

feature6_1_retrieve.json.description: A scoped registry that stores a single value per type key. Looking up a type before anything is registered yields nothing; after registering a value it can be retrieved by the same type key. The output reports the lookup result before and after registration.

feature6_2_scoped_zone.json.description: Exposes the active registry only within a dynamically scoped execution region. Outside any region the ambient instance is absent; while running inside the region the registry's instance becomes the ambient one and its registered values are visible; after the region exits the ambient instance is absent again.

feature7_1_load_by_id.json.description: Loads a saved code document by its identifier over an HTTP boundary. A valid identifier returns the document and its primary file content; an unknown identifier and an over-quota response are surfaced as normalized error categories. The output reports the requested URL, the HTTP status observed, and either the primary file content or the error category.

cases.expected_output:
request=https://api.github.com/gists/12345678901234567890123456789012
status=200
main.dart=This is some dart code!

feature7_2_load_by_sample.json.description: Loads a documentation code sample by its sample identifier from the samples endpoint. A valid sample returns a single primary file; an unknown sample is surfaced as a normalized not-found category. The output reports the requested URL, the HTTP status, and either the primary file length or the error category.

feature7_3_load_from_repo.json.description: Loads a multi-file exercise from a source repository by first fetching a metadata descriptor and then fetching each listed file. The descriptor supplies the exercise name and the file list; an optional branch reference is appended to every request. A file may declare an alternate path that is used to fetch its content while keeping its logical name. The output reports the first requested URL, the HTTP status, the exercise name, and the content of a selected file.

cases.expected_output:
request=https://api.github.com/repos/owner/repo/contents/basic/dartpad_metadata.yaml
status=200
description=A Dart Exercise
main.dart=this is main.dart

request=https://api.github.com/repos/owner/repo/contents/alt_branch/dartpad_metadata.yaml?ref=some_branch
status=200
description=A Dart Exercise
main.dart=this is main.dart

request=https://api.github.com/repos/owner/repo/contents/alternate_path/dartpad_metadata.yaml
status=200
description=A Dart Exercise
test.dart=this is test.dart

request=https://api.github.com/repos/owner/repo/contents/unnecessary_file/dartpad_metadata.yaml
status=200
description=A Dart Exercise
unnecessary.txt=this is unnecessary.txt

feature7_4_repo_errors.json.description: Surfaces normalized error categories when an exercise cannot be loaded: a descriptor that is not valid structured data, a descriptor missing its file list, mode, or name, a descriptor that points at a file which does not exist, a descriptor path that itself does not exist, and an over-quota response. The output reports the first requested URL, the HTTP status, and the normalized error category.

feature8_1_dirty_tracking.json.description: An editable overlay on a code document that tracks whether it diverges from its backing document. It starts clean, becomes dirty after a field is changed, becomes clean again if the field is restored to its original value, and becomes clean when a new backing document is adopted. The output reports the dirty flag after each step.

feature8_2_create_gist.json.description: Produces a new immutable code document that captures the current edited state of an editable overlay. The output reports the field value carried into the newly created document.

feature8_3_clone.json.description: Creates an independent deep copy of a code document, preserving its identifier and description. The output reports the cloned document's identifier and description.
